key_insight: A viscoplastic finite-element damage model can predict fracture initiation and failure in additively manufactured polymer TPMS lattices using stored elastic energy and equivalent plastic strain.
engineering_relevance: This enables more reliable in silico design of lightweight architected polymer lattices for energy absorption, toughness, and load-bearing applications.
method: Built on a high-fidelity rate-dependent viscoplastic constitutive model implemented in Abaqus/Explicit via UMAT.
Introduced a damage initiation criterion for amorphous polymers based on stored elastic energy and equivalent plastic strain.
Implemented fracture evolution with a gradient-damage framework and validated it against experiments.
evidence_signal: The model was quantitatively and qualitatively validated against experiments on a unit cell under non-monotonic loading and on lattices under tension.
watch_item: The approach depends on calibrated material parameters and specialized Abaqus implementations, so transferability to other polymers, print conditions, or TPMS geometries may require revalidation.
paper_title: Modeling damage and fracture in additively manufactured polymeric triply periodic minimal surface lattices
